note: The skill is a generic prompt-level accuracy/caution directive, and its likely effects on coding are most visible on deterministic tasks where small reasoning, implementation, or repair mistakes flip pass/fail outcomes. I prioritized code repair, competitive programming, and realistic SWE tasks with executable validation over factual QA or broad multimodal reasoning.

selections.reason: Python code repair tasks are compact, deterministic, and highly sensitive to off-by-one errors, missed edge cases, and overconfident edits, so an added caution directive could measurably help or slow/overcomplicate fixes.

Real bug-fix tasks with human-validated issues and test-based grading expose whether extra caution improves repository navigation and patch precision or causes excessive conservatism and incomplete fixes.
Algorithmic coding tasks have unambiguous pass/fail tests and are error-prone in reasoning, implementation details, and edge cases, making them a strong probe for a 'be careful' prompt intervention.
Small single-defect repair tasks in Python and Java are deterministic and mistake-sensitive, though many are simpler than full SWE tasks, so the effect may be easier but less representative.
Challenging Python programming tasks with reward-based verification should reveal changes in precision and edge-case handling, especially if the skill affects solution planning or test awareness.
Polyglot editing tasks broaden coverage across languages and editing patterns, useful for detecting regressions from verbosity or hesitation, though effects may be noisier across heterogeneous tasks.
Large JavaScript/TypeScript bug-fix set with realistic issues can reveal coding-agent pass-rate shifts, but generated task provenance makes it slightly less clean than verified real-world sets.
